coredata.dc:title: Recent advances of carbon dot-based memristors: Mechanisms, devices, and applications
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yanli
Hao, Haotian
Chen, Lin
Yang, Yongzhen
coredata.dc:description: Memristors are considered to hold promising applications in the fields of resistive random-access memory and synaptic bionic devices owing to their unique nonlinear electrical characteristics. Stability of performances is critical for memristors, in which functional layer materials are key. As novel functional layer materials, carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm. Owing to their distinctive features such as excellent thermal stability, high electrical conductivity, favorable dispersion and quantum effect, they can be used as functional layer materials for memristors either individually or in combination with other resistive materials to achieve satisfactory memristor regulation. This review summarizes the recent advances of CD-based memristors, including memristive mechanisms, devices, and applications. Firstly, the common memristive mechanisms of CD-based memristors are categorized as metal conductive filament mechanism, electron trapping and detrapping mechanism and oxygen vacancy conductive filament mechanism. Secondly, CDs-based memristors are classified into digital and analog types, and the effect of CDs on improving the performance of different types of memristors is further investigated. Finally, the current application fields of CD-based memristors at the present stage are reviewed, and the prospects for their development are prospected. This work is instructive for understanding the research progress of CD-based memristors and exploring the role of CDs in memristors, which brings the possibility of obtaining high-performance memristors for achieving better neuromorphic computing and data storage.

head.ce:title: Recent advances of carbon dot-based memristors: Mechanisms, devices, and applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Memristors are considered to hold promising applications in the fields of resistive random-access memory and synaptic bionic devices owing to their unique nonlinear electrical characteristics. Stability of performances is critical for memristors, in which functional layer materials are key. As novel functional layer materials, carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm. Owing to their distinctive features such as excellent thermal stability, high electrical conductivity, favorable dispersion and quantum effect, they can be used as functional layer materials for memristors either individually or in combination with other resistive materials to achieve satisfactory memristor regulation. This review summarizes the recent advances of CD-based memristors, including memristive mechanisms, devices, and applications. Firstly, the common memristive mechanisms of CD-based memristors are categorized as metal conductive filament mechanism, electron trapping and detrapping mechanism and oxygen vacancy conductive filament mechanism. Secondly, CDs-based memristors are classified into digital and analog types, and the effect of CDs on improving the performance of different types of memristors is further investigated. Finally, the current application fields of CD-based memristors at the present stage are reviewed, and the prospects for their development are prospected. This work is instructive for understanding the research progress of CD-based memristors and exploring the role of CDs in memristors, which brings the possibility of obtaining high-performance memristors for achieving better neuromorphic computing and data storage.

This review summarizes the recent advances of carbon dot-based memristors, including the memristive mechanisms, devices, and application fields of carbon dot-based memristors.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Memristors are considered to be the fourth basic circuit element in addition to resistors, capacitors, and inductors owing to their dependence of the resistance on the quantity amount of electricity passed through them [1]. They have been extensively studied for applications in non-volatile logic operation and information storage because of their fast switching speed, low power consumption, high storage density, and the complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) compatibility [2–4]. In addition, memristors have unique nonlinear electrical properties, and their conductance state can be continuously modulated by an external electric field, which is recognized to be the perfect devices for deep learning by simulating the human brain [5–7].

Memristors are sandwich-like structures consisting of electrodes at both ends and a functional layer, in which the functional layer material directly affects the resistive performances of devices. Functional layer materials mainly include metal oxides [8–10] (e.g., HfO2-x, ZnO, and Al2O3), carbon nanomaterials [11,12] (e.g., multi-walled carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide), and polymers [13,14] (e.g., polyvinylpyrrolidone and polymethylmethacrylate). Memristors based on the above materials show great potential in image information storage and optoelectronic logic operation. However, in terms of device performances, there are still issues such as poor resistance stability, large switching voltage dispersion and small retention characteristics [15,16]. In recent years, researchers have focused on improving the performances by optimizing, doping and compounding the functional layer materials of memristors [17,18].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterials, have the characteristics of excellent thermal stability, charge storage capacity, conductivity, fluorescence properties, modifiable energy level bandgap, and are widely available source of raw materials [19–22]. CDs show great potential in the application of functional layer materials. CDs are generally spherical or spheroidal structures consisting of a carbon core and a surface state, with a size of less than 10 nm [23,24]. The carbon core is mainly composed of sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon, and the surface state contains a large number of functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups [25,26]. Currently, according to the different carbon core microstructures, CDs applied in memristors can be divided into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [27,28]. Because of some oxidization that may occur during the preparation of GQDs, strictly speaking, they are graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) [29]. GOQDs usually contains oxygen functional groups on the surface compared with GQDs.

Moreover, doping different elements in CDs grants them different functions. For instance, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) possessing outstanding photocatalytic characteristics can separate photogenerated electron-hole (e − and h +) pairs, resulting in more electrons that can shorten electron tunneling distance and form more controllable conductive filaments, which can provide a reference methodology for the regulation of optoelectronic memristive properties [30,31]. Oxidized carbon quantum dots (OCQDs) can regulate the energy level barriers and effectively reduce the power consumption of devices and so on [32]. Nowadays, researchers have explored the composite of CDs with conventional resistive materials to form composite films as the functional layer materials for memristors. It is found that the performances of memristors have been significantly improved after doping with CDs, which include the decrease of switching voltage, the improvement of retention time, switching current ratio, and endurance. Therefore, memristors based on CDs and their composites have attracted extensive attention from researchers.

Herein, this review aims to summarize and conclude recent advances of CD-based memristors. It mainly includes the memristive mechanisms, devices, and application fields of CD-based memristors, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the main memristive mechanisms are presented in three aspects: metal conductive filament mechanism, electron trapping and detrapping mechanism, and oxygen vacancy conductive filament mechanism. Then, depending on the discrete or continuous properties of the resistive switching, the memristors are classified into two types: digital and analog. The role of CDs on the performance improvement of different types of memristors is further explored. At last, the prospects for the applications of CD-based memristors in the fields of information storage encryption and artificial synapse network are concluded. Meanwhile, the future of CD-based memristors is envisioned to provide assistance and inspiration for obtaining memristors with more stable performances.

CD-based memristors usually have a "sandwich" structure of top electrode/functional layer/bottom electrode (Fig. 2 a). The electrodes can significantly affect the memristive mechanisms primarily by directly participating in redox reactions or acting as oxygen reservoirs or charge carrier transport paths. Top electrodes are generally active metals or inert metals (e.g., Ag, AgNWs, Al, Au, and TiN) and bottom electrodes are generally inert metals, some metal oxides (e.g., Pt, Au, indium tin oxide (ITO), F-doped SnO2 (FTO)), and gelatin. The functional layer materials mainly include metal oxides, carbon nanomaterials and polymers. Metal oxides are prevalent in memristors because of their simple structures, high thermal stability, and easy compatibility with CMOS process. Carbon nanomaterials hold the comprehensive merits of high electrical conductivity, thermal and mechanical properties, and easy integration with the CMOS technology. Polymer materials offer high dielectric constants and large device switching ratios, which provide a basis for the preparation of multilevel storage and flexible memristor devices. Therefore, the structures of memristors are often different.

Memristors undergo the transition of different resistance states by applying a voltage, i.e., high resistive state (HRS) and low resistive state (LRS). The transition from HRS to LRS is commonly defined as set process, and the transition from LRS to HRS is reset process [33]. These resistive behaviors are accompanied by the transport of electrons, ions and other charges, and are affected by a variety of factors such as device structure, resistive materials, electrodes and preparation process. Memristors composed of different electrodes and functional layer materials exhibit different resistive characteristics, and their corresponding memristive mechanisms also show the difference.

CDs as functional layer materials can improve the charge transport mechanism and conductive properties in memristors. As a result, the performances of switching voltage (V SET and V RESET), switching current ratio (I ON/I OFF), retention time, endurance, and power consumption can be effectively improved [34,35]. CDs with different microstructures paly different roles in the devices. GQDs are composed of edge-functionalized nano-sized graphene fragments, and their carbon core consists of a stable graphene carbon skeleton, in which the quantum well structure makes GQDs an excellent charge trapping medium. Therefore, GQDs can effectively regulate the transition between HRS and LRS of memristors. In addition, GQDs are highly crystallized, present a π-conjugated system, and have better co-planarity and neat atomic arrangement, which show better electrical conductivity, thus reducing the power consumption of memristors [33,36,37]. CQDs with crystalline sp2 hybridized carbon as core have relatively high crystallinity and carbonization degree. Owing to large electron transfer efficiency, CQDs can improve the uniformity and retention characteristics of memristors [33]. CNDs with an amorphous sp3 hybridized carbon as core have more surface defects compared with CQDs [38]. CNDs can provide more possibilities for surface passivation and functionalization of various polymeric, organic or inorganic materials, which is beneficial for matching the energy levels between different resistive materials, promoting electron transport and decreasing the power consumption of memristors [33]. In contrast, GQDs are more conducive to optimizing device performance than other two types of CDs. Because of the stable graphene carbon skeleton, unique quantum well structure, large π-conjugated system, and better co-planarity, GQDs can better improve the device memristive stability [27,28]. Therefore, the introduction of CDs can modulate the resistive characteristics of memristors, affect the memristive mechanism and enhance the performances of devices.

Currently, memristors based on different types of CDs and their composite materials show different memristive mechanisms, which are generally recognized as metal conductive filament mechanism, electron trapping and detrapping mechanism and oxygen vacancy conductive filament mechanism (Fig. 2b, c and d) [39–42].

Metal conductive filament mechanism is one of the reasons for the resistive phenomenon in CDs and their composite-based memristors [43,44]. Top electrodes are generally active metals (e.g., Ag, Cu, and Al) and bottom electrodes are generally inert metals or some metal oxide (e.g., Pt, Au, and ITO) [45–48]. By applying a positive voltage to the top electrode, the active metals undergo an oxidation reaction and generate metal cations. They migrate in the functional layer to the bottom electrode and are reduced to metal atoms by reduction reactions. With the accumulation of metal atoms, a conductive pathway consisting of metal atoms, known as conductive filaments (CFs), is formed in the functional layer. When the reverse voltage is applied, the metal atoms are oxidized to metal cations again. They migrate towards the top electrode and the CFs break. The transition between HRS and LRS occurs accompanied by the formation and rupture of CFs in the functional layer (Fig. 2b). Size and number of CFs determine the performances of memristors. However, uncontrollable diffusion of metal ions causes disordered growth of dendritic CFs, eventually, resulting in poor device stability and endurance [49,50].

In fact, the formation of CFs could be controlled by doping and structural optimization of functional layer materials to improve the stability and endurance of memristors. As the electric field lines converge near the CDs to form a tip electric field, it can guide the metal ions to migrate along the direction of the electric field. Therefore, CDs have the local electric field enhancement effect, which can fix the migration direction of CFs in the middle part of the functional layer, making the memristive performances more stable [51–53]. Chen et al. [29] fabricated Al/MgO–GOQDs/ITO memristors based on the MgO-GOQD hybrid films. It exhibited ultra-low operating voltages and highly uniform switching parameters. The memristive mechanism was found to be attributed to the formation and rupture of metallic Mg CFs in the MgO-GOQDs layer. GOQDs acted as trapping centers for carriers in the MgO-GOQDs layer and generated enhanced local electric field to guide the Mg CF's growth. Li et al. [54] proposed a method of embedding CQDs in polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) as a functional layer material to produce the Ag/PMMA&CQDs/FTO memristors. In addition, a simulation model was developed using COMSOL software to explore the effect of CQDs on the internal electric field of PMMA. It was proved that the introduction of CQDs could promote the ordered growth of CFs. CQDs would form a local electric field, which fixes the Ag+ migration direction relatively in the middle part of the PMMA films. Memristors with excellent switching stability and endurance were obtained.

Electron trapping effect refers to the trapping process of charge carriers from the electrodes into the trapping centers, which usually arises from the metal nanoparticles and electron acceptors (e.g. carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, organic electron-withdrawing groups) [55]. When all the defect sites are filled with charge carriers under external electric field, memristors can be transformed from HRS to LRS. The mechanism is mainly related to impurities and defects in functional layers. They introduce new energy levels that can act as electron traps for trapping and detrapping, thus allowing memristors to undergo a resistive transition. In this mechanism, the electrons mostly move in a space charge limited conduction (SCLC) model to achieve the transition between HRS and LRS of memristors [56,57].

CDs are introduced into memristors to act as electron trapping centers by the abundance of defect sites on their surface. CDs may enhance the ability to trap electrons and generate conductive channels in the functional layer similar to CFs, improving the performances of devices (Fig. 2c) [58]. Lin et al. [59] constructed a photo-tunable flexible organic memristor device with the structure of Al/PVP-NCQDs/ITO by introducing NCQDs nanoparticles into polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) films. If the positive bias voltage was applied on the top electrode, charge carriers were injected from the ITO electrodes and trapped in the NCQDs. When the voltage exceeds the V SET, the trapped charge carriers were released to form conductive paths within the PVP-NCQDs films, causing the transformation of resistive states. On the basis of the above studies, Zeng et al. [60] demonstrated that the memristive mechanism of Al/PVP-NCQDs/ITO was consistent with the SCLC mechanism by I-V fitting curve and in situ Kelvin probe force microscopy. When forward bias was applied to the top electrode, memristors presented the HRS owing to the small voltage. With the voltage increasing, the polar groups and defect sites on the surface of NCQDs could trap more electrons. When all the defect sites in the functional layer were filled with electrons, the electrons that continued to be injected could move freely between the two electrodes to form a conductive path, thus switching to the LRS.

Oxygen vacancy conductive filament mechanism is distinguished from metal conducting filament mechanism, which usually appears in devices with inert metals as the top and bottom electrodes, and different functional layer materials, typically oxide. After application of the positive voltage, oxygen ions in the oxide detach from the crystal lattice. Positively charged oxygen vacancies are left at the original position and migrate to the top electrode by the effect of electric field. With increasing applied voltage, the oxygen vacancies produced gradually increase. Thus, an oxygen vacancy CF connecting the top electrode and the bottom electrode is established. When the reverse voltage is exerted, the oxygen ions return to the lattice, and the oxygen vacancy CF is broken, producing the transition between HRS and LRS (Fig. 2d).

Lin et al. [61] constructed memristors with the structure of reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/GONCQDs/graphene. Photoreduction of graphene oxide (GO) and NCQDs nanocomposites demonstrated that all-carbon memristor synapses displayed analog type resistive behaviors. Because the photocatalytic reduction reaction occurred near the NCQDs, locally conducting RGO domains were obtained. Under the effect of electric field, oxygen ions migrate to form multiple weak oxygen vacancy CFs. Thus, a continuum of resistive switching was obtained to construct analog type memristors.

In addition to the three main mechanisms mentioned above, there are also Pool-Frenkel mechanism, Schottky barrier layer mechanism, Fowler-Nordheim tunnelling mechanism, and other methods of electron transport [12,62,63]. The resistive characteristics of CD-based memristors are not only affected by one of them but affected by the combined effect of multiple mechanisms. The memristive mechanism is affected by a variety of factors, for instance, device structure, resistive materials, electrodes, and preparation process. There is no one universal guideline to clarify the memristive mechanism. Therefore, the intrinsic memristive regularities of CD-based memristors need to be continuously explored for better optimization of their performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance factors affecting memristors by CDs structure
ce:section.ce:para:
By reviewing the relevant literature, it is observed that the following properties of CDs are required if memristors with excellent performances could be obtained:
(1)
Uniform size distribution and excellent dispersion. The distribution of CDs in the functional layer influences performances of memristors. CDs with small sizes and uniform distribution can easily dispersed uniformly in other resistive materials. Thus, the functional layer has lower roughness, better flatness and uniform thickness, which plays an essential role in improving devices' performances.
(2)
High degree of crystallinity. Higher crystallinity indicates that CDs have relatively complete sp2 conjugated structures. Electrons can flow through the sp2 conducting pathway, which facilitates charge transfer and enhances electron mobility within CDs. That could decrease switching voltage and improve devices stability.
(3)
Abundant surface defects. For electron trapping and detrapping mechanisms, polar groups and defects on the surface of CDs can regulate the generation and recombination of electron-hole pairs. Type and number of defects determine the ability of CDs to capture electrons. Surface defects can act as electron trapping centers, and the trapped electrons generate conductive channels in the functional layer, which facilitates conduction and reduces power consumption. CDs composites are potentially feasible for the realization of artificial synaptic bionic devices based on charge-capture type.

Therefore, size, degree of crystallinity and surface state of CDs can be optimized by selecting appropriate reaction feedstocks and modulating the preparation process. By improving the dispersion of CDs in other resistive materials, efficient memristors can be obtained.

Based on the discrete or continuous properties of the resistive switching, memristors can be classified into digital type and analog type [64]. Digital type memristors have a discrete type of resistive switching, including HRS and LRS, which correspond to the "0″ and "1″ states in data storage, respectively. They show significant potential in new memory devices owing to their ability to data storage [65,66]. Analog type memristors exhibit a continuum change in resistive switching, which are quite similar to the synapses of brain. Therefore, they can simulate a variety of basic synaptic behaviors [5,67]. They are also considered to have good application prospects in neuromorphic computing and artificial intelligence [68,69]. As functional layer materials, CDs and their composites can effectively enhance device performances and play an influential role in digital and analog type memristors.

Nowadays, CDs are composited with metal oxides, carbon nanomaterials or polymers as functional layer materials for digital type memristors, as shown in Table 1 . According to some reports, CDs with local electric field enhancement effects often perform the well modulation effects on memristance [70,71]. Because of tip electric field, electric field strength around CDs is higher than other regions. It is beneficial to guide the nucleation and migration direction of metal ions or oxygen vacancies. Hereby, random growth of CFs between the two electrodes is avoided [72]. It creates the possibility of obtaining devices with low switching voltage, long retention time and high stability and reproducibility.

Memristors using metal oxides as functional layer materials have attracted extensive attention by researchers owing to the advantages of simple structure, easy to control components, convenient fabrication and compatibility with CMOS process [73]. Furthermore, there are many benefits in device preparation and the realization of a memory-computing integrated chip. It was discovered that doping CDs could effectively improve the stability, parameter fluctuation and power consumption of the devices.

Yan et al. [72] prepared memristors structured with Ag/Zr0.5Hf0.5O2(ZHO)/ GOQDs/ZHO/Pt (Fig. 3 a and b). Under DC scanning voltage, the memristors exhibited the typical bipolar resistive switching behavior. The V SET /V RESET could be significantly reduced, the value distribution was more concentrated (Fig. 3c), and the retention time was considerably enhanced (Fig. 3d). In addition, the device's switching speed (14 ns/50 ns) (Fig. 3e and f), and power consumption (2 × 10−5 W/3.9 × 10−7 W) were further improved. It can be attributed to the local electric field enhancement effect at the GOQD sites, which could direct the nucleation and growth direction of CFs and promote the formation of CFs by shortening the effective distance of Ag+ movement.

Qi et al. [70] inserted CQDs as the localized electric field regulators into memristors based on hafnium oxide (HfO2-x) to achieve uniform operation of multistage memristors. The introduction of CQDs led to improved uniformity of device performances (6.4 %/4.9 % reduction in V SET /V RESET relative errors and 27.8 %/11.1 % reduction in HRS/LRS relative errors). It also had desirable retention performance (105 s). The reduced CQDs in the functional layer featured high electrical conductivity, which was similar to metal nanoparticles. CQDs could enhance the localized electric field around them, inducing the formation of CFs with simple structures. Thus, the device uniformity and retention characteristics were greatly improved [74,75].

Carbon nanomaterials are a class of low-dimensional materials, including fullerenes, graphene, carbon nanotubes and nanodiamonds in addition to CDs [76]. The physical limitations of miniaturization of silicon-based integrated circuits and heat dissipation problems have also greatly limited the development of conventional von Neumann computers [77]. Among many information storage materials, carbon nanomaterials are recognized as the most promising alternative to traditional silicon-based materials for electronic applications. This has significant implications for the rapid development of future memristors [78]. It has been found that CDs are more beneficial for improving performances of other carbon nanomaterial-based memristors owing to their unique optoelectronic properties, such as photocatalytic effect and electron trapping and detrapping effect.

GO has a structure similar to graphene. Because of the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups between layers of graphene, it has excellent performances of flexibility and mechanical stability [3]. Qi et al. [32] enhanced the retention of CFs by embedding OCQDs into Al/GO/ITO memristors. Compared with hydroxyl group-dominated GO films, OCQDs introduced more epoxy groups, which could increase the migration energy barrier (Ea), leading to a higher degree of oxidation around the GO trapping center. This decreased the conductivity of the functional layer and improved the charge trapping effects. As a result, memristors based on OCQDs-GO nanocomposite films exhibited stable bipolar resistive switching characteristics and excellent retention time (104 s) at small compliance currents (100 µA). Ren et al. [71] investigated the bipolar resistive switching behaviors and magnetic switching effects in Ag/GQDs: GO/ITO devices (left inset of Fig. 4 a) by incorporating 8 wt% of GQDs into GO films. It was found that the switching voltage was greatly reduced (Fig. 4a) and more uniformly distributed after the introduction of GQDs (Fig. 4b). The switching ratio and saturation magnetization strength of the GQD: GO-based memristors were about 20 and 2.2 times higher, respectively, than that of pure GO (Fig. 4c and d). GQDs promoted the formation of sp2 filaments along themselves in the presence of the local electric field enhancement effect, thus reducing the random formation of CFs. Most significantly, GQDs contributed the same magnetic order to the localized detached magnetic moments, thereby generating global magnetic behaviors in GQDs: GO composite films in the HRS at room temperature. This study provided new way for GO-based bifunctional memristor devices with high-resistance memories and large magnetization modulation.

Owing to the ultraviolet (UV) sensitivity and resistance modulation ability of GQDs, Wang et al. [37] prepared Al/MWCNT: GQDs/ITO memristors with rewritable nonvolatile properties via composite films consisting of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and GQDs. The switching current ratios of the devices were tuned in two ways. The first way utilized the sensitivity of GQDs to UV light. As the illumination time increased to 30 min, the maximum switching current ratio dropped sharply from 4.58 × 103 to 10.77. This may be due to the reduction of oxygen-containing groups, as well as the increase of sp2 carbon atoms, resulting in a decrease in trap concentration and switching current ratio. Another approach was to take advantage of the charge trapping properties of GQDs. More oxygen-containing groups would be introduced into MWCNTs to increase the energy band barriers, thus elevating the electron trapping depth of the composite film and the initial resistance of the device. The switching current ratio rose to 3.3 × 103 when the mass ratio of GQDs reached 50 %. This study laid the theoretical foundation for obtaining logic memories with faster transition speeds and lower power consumption.

Ai et al. [79] prepared TiN/CISA-CDs/ITO/Mica memristors by two-dimensional effective encapsulation of individual CDs to form flexible CDs strips (CISA-CDs) as functional layer materials. The devices endowed outstanding data storage capabilities, retention characteristics (30 days) and fast photoelectric response characteristics (5.5 ns). Two-dimensional assembly became more stable with the reduction of specific surface area. The surface of CDs here was coated with ligands, and the ligands were attached to the surface during the assembly process to form a stable morphology. The surface ligands can effectively prevent the direct erosion of the carbon core by water and oxygen, thus greatly strengthening the stability. This work established an essential structure-property relationship between the function of individual CDs and the electronic properties of their components, which provided guidance for the construction of efficient and stable CD-based memristors.

Polymer materials possess the advantages of good film formation, low cost, high ductility, and flexibility. At the same time, these materials offer high dielectric constants and large device switching ratios, which provide a basis for the preparation of multilevel storage and flexible memristor devices [80]. A wide variety of polymer materials have been reported, mainly including PMMA, PVP, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), silk, and chitosan [81–83].

Li et al. [54] proposed a method to fabricate memristors by embedding CQDs into PMMA. The device structure was shown in Fig. 5 a. The device performances were optimum when the doping amount of CQDs was 0.5 %. They exhibited excellent switching repeatability (Fig. 5b), low switching voltage (1.1 V/−1.025 V), acceptable switching ratio (150), reliable endurance (200 cycles) (Fig. 5c), retention time (104 s), and stable forming voltage (Fig. 5d). CQDs acted as the carrier trapping centers, showing the local electric field enhancement effect, which was greatly higher than their vicinity in the PMMA film. This resulted in the Ag+ migration direction was relatively fixed in the middle part of the PMMA film, which reduced the randomness of CFs formation. This was the main reason for the stable performances of memristors.

NCQDs, as a photoresponsive nanomaterial, have small size, high electron transfer efficiency and excellent photocatalytic properties. Incorporating NCQDs into the functional layer may tune and improve the performances of memristors [84]. Lin et al. [59] introduced NCQDs nanoparticles into PVP films to prepare phototuned flexible organic memristors. After tuning the UV light irradiation, the switching voltage was reduced (0.5 V), the V SET distribution was more concentrated, and the stability was improved (up to 104 bending times). When the PVP-NCQDs films were irradiated with UV light, electrons and holes were generated in NCQDs. The holes reacted with the surface-adsorbed water to form oxygen and protons. While the electrons transferred from the NCQDs to PVP to dissociate of oxygen functional group, thus causing partial discharges of the conducting sp2 domains around the NCQDs and further reducing the switching voltage.

Meng et al. [82] constructed ITO/CNDs-PVA/Ag memristors. The device possessed bistable resistive switching behaviors with high I ON/I OFF ratio (105) and low switching voltage (1 V/−3 V), with retention time up to 3.4 × 103 s. Under a reading pulse of 0.2 V, the programming states could be stable and read up to 104 cycles. This could be attributed to the charge trapping and detrapping behavior of CNDs in the PVA film. As a charge capture site, CNDs could effectively capture and release electrons with abundant surface functional groups.

In summary, owing to the local electric field enhancement effect of CDs, CFs in digital type memristors are regularly grown and directional migrated. In Table 1, it can be found that nearly half of the reported CD-based memristors have switching voltages lower than 1.0 V, while the switching voltages of other memristors based on metal oxides, carbon nanomaterials, and polymers are higher. In addition, CD-based memristors also present reasonable switching current ratio, relatively long retention time, and high endurance. This could potentially assist in exploring digital type memristors for information storage [59]. Besides, the introduction of heteroatom-doped CDs brings new ideas for the enhancement of device performances. As an example, OCQDs could enable to increase Ea and decrease the recombination effect in the functional layer to help enhancing of LRS retention, thus resulting in an improvement in the data retention. Utilizing the superior photocatalytic ability of NCQDs can raise the memristive performances, which provides new possibilities for the realization of the optoelectronic integrated memristors.

The resistance of memristors to current depends on the amount of current that has previously flowed through them. After the electrical power failure, memristors can still "memorize" current flowed through them. Memristors with non-linear memory properties can remember large information as a biological nerve cell does, which shows potential in acquiring autonomous learning capabilities [85,86]. Synapses are used to transfer information among different neurons of biological organisms. Memristors with "sandwich" structure can precisely simulate the "pre-synapse-synaptic cleft-post-synapse " structure of synapses (as shown in Fig. 6 ) [21]. Conduction can be dynamically regulated by voltage spikes [87]. Hence, analog type memristors can simulate a wide range of basic synaptic behaviors. For example, short-term plasticity (STP), long-term plasticity (LTP), short-term depression (STD), long-time depression (LTD), spike-timing-dependent- plasticity (STDP) function, paired-pulse facilitation (PPF) function, and excitatory postsynaptic current (EPSC). Analog type memristors are considered to be well-promised in neuromorphic computing and artificial intelligence [88–90]. CDs composited with metal oxides, carbon nanomaterials or polymers as functional layer materials have likewise received much attention in analog type memristors.

Because functional layer contains multiple CFs with localized defects, the resistive switching of analog type memristors is in a continuous manner. The local electric field enhancement effect of CDs can contribute to forming multiple CFs. Thus, the disordered growth and migration of CFs can be reduced, and the transformation of digital type memristors into analog type memristors can also be realized. Abundant defect sites on the surface of CDs could assist in trapping charges and reduce tunneling distance between charges. Therefore, it is prone to form multiple conductive channels, which makes the resistive switching change continuously, thus simulating a variety of synaptic behaviors. At present, CDs have been composited with metal oxides, carbon nanomaterials, and polymers for analog type memristors, as shown in Table 2 .

Composite of CDs with metal oxides (MgO, Al2O3, FeOx, Ga2O3, ZHO, HfO2, etc.) can be employed as functional layer materials. They can provide richer synaptic plasticity behaviors for memristors, and improve device performances effectively.

Chen et al. [29] prepared Al/MgO-GOQDs/ITO memory devices (Fig. 7 a) using MgO-GOQDs hybrid films. Compared with the memristors without GOQDs, the switching voltage was reduced by about 60 % and the distribution was narrowed by about 80 %. In addition, the memristors had excellent synaptic functions, such as PPF (Fig. 7b), STDP (Fig. 7c), STP, and LTP. The introduced GOQDs could serve as a trapping center for carriers. The carriers captured by the GOQDs generate a local electric field enhancement effect in the MgO-GOQDs composite films, which promoted the formation and rupture of Mg CFs.

Pei et al. [20] proposed a novel memristor based on Pd/CQDs/Ga2O3/Pt. Its structure was shown in Fig. 7d and e. The device demonstrated a significantly centralized switching voltage distribution (V SET /V RESET of 1.7 V/−0.06 V), low power consumption (SET/RESET of 1.2 × 107 W/4.15 × 10−5 W), high stability (4.5 × 104 s, operating period of 106) and heat resistance (420 K). The device reliably simulated LTD, PPF, four types of STDP learning, and Pavlovian associative learning functions (Fig. 7f). The local electric field enhancement effect of CQDs improved the switching dispersion of memristor. More stable and uniform performances were obtained owing to the low diffusivity of carbon cluster ions in the oxide and the controllable growth and nucleation positions of carbon CFs.

Xu et al. [91] developed an ultrathin electronic synapse with Ag/Al2O3/GQDs/Al2O3/ITO structure, which had excellent stability and low power consumption. GQDs served as electron traps to regulate electron transport based on Fowler-Nordheim tunneling. Moreover, GQDs' ability to trap electrons led to gradual changes in conductance. Thereby, basic synaptic functions such as LTP, STD and PPF were simulated. Wang et al. [92] introduced GQDs into memristors structured as Pt/FeOx/Pt. GQDs could modify the energy band gap and boost the tunneling current. Also, GQDs could bind with abundant and easily detached oxygen functional groups, decrease the randomness of oxygen vacancy formation and migration, and improve the reproducibility of the analog resistive states. Compared with control samples, the GQDs-based memristors exhibited highly reproducible analog resistance states. The switching voltage was reduced by 40 % and the distribution was also narrowed by 85 %, and the distribution of HRS and LRS was significantly reduced by almost 90 %. These GQDs-based devices could act as artificial synapses with controllable changes in synaptic weights. By reducing the training epochs and lowering the probability of programming errors, the neural network was enabled to have an accurate and efficient learning capability. Therefore, it had facilitated the further development of artificial synapses for brain-inspired computing systems.

Researchers have found that doping CDs into GO films can enhance the device performances, and the simulation of multiple synaptic plasticity behaviors of memristors can be achieved by applying optical signal stimulation.

Lin et al. [61] obtained RGO/GONCQDs/graphene memristors by introducing NCQDs into GO sheets (Fig. 8 a), which was achieved by a simple and mild photoreduction method. It provided a new approach to the realization of an all-carbon memristive synapse. These synaptic devices exhibited synaptic learning capability (EPSC, PPF, and STDP, etc.). By UV irradiation, electron (e −) and hole (h +) pairs were generated in the NCQDs. Owing to the adsorption of H2O molecules on the surface of GO, h + reacted with H2O to form O2 and H +. The photogenerated e − were transferred from the NCQDs to the GO sheets, where they reacted with oxygen functional groups to locally reduce the GO (Fig. 8b). The local RGO domains were generated. Because of the local electric field enhancement effect, the multiple weak CFs preferentially grew around the RGO domains in the GO films. Thus, the analog type memristive behaviors with continuous properties could be demonstrated.

In addition to CDs and their composites, studies of single CDs as functional layer materials, as CQDs and N-GOQDs, are gradually reported. Yu et al. [93] constructed memristors structured with Au/CQDs/ITO. The test results showed that the coefficient of variation of the switching voltage could be as low as 1.551 % and 0.083 %, which confirmed the uniform switching characteristics. The devices could simulate important biological behaviors such as Pavlov's dog reflection, EPSC, LTP, LTD, and STDP. It was supposed that the memristive mechanism of the CQDs-based memristors was caused by the CFs resulting from the hybridization state transitions of the sp2 and sp3 carbon domains. This avoided the disadvantage of uncontrolled nucleation sites causing random formation of CFs. Ai et al. [94] developed Ag/N-GOQDs/Pt memristors, which had low switching voltage (0.141 V), large I ON/I OFF ratio (106), and long endurance (5 × 103). Under UV excitation, NGOQDs could separate electron-hole pairs. Excited electrons could trigger the formation of Ag CFs from Ag+, enhancing the conductivity of memristors. The UV light source was analogous to the heterosynaptic neuro-modulatory axon, which was capable of modulating synaptic plasticity (EPSC, STP to LTP transitions). These discoveries were a step forward for realizing higher bandwidth synaptic modulation as a future hardware-based neural network application.

Polymer materials have properties of flexibility as well as film-forming properties. They are also applied in analog type memristors. Composite with CDs as a novel functional layer materials can enable memristors to simulate a variety of synaptic plasticity behaviors. Moreover, it also provides a feasible solution for constructing flexible and transparent memristors in the future.

Zeng et al. [60] constructed the Al/PVP-NCQDs/ITO memristors by regulating the NCQDs doping concentration (Fig. 8c). The NCQDs could increase the charge trapping centers and promote the formation of multiple conductive pathways. This led to a continuous change in the conductivity of the memristors with analog characteristics. The devices could emulate multiple important synaptic functions, including EPSC (Fig. 8d), PPF (Fig. 8e and f), STP to LTP transition (Fig. 8g and h), and STDP (Fig. 8i).

Owing to the local electric field enhancement effect and electron trapping and detrapping effect of CDs, memristors typically have excellent storage memory properties. As shown in Table 2, these properties include high uniformity, stability, and uniform switching voltage distribution. In addition, CD-based memristors can achieve various basic biological synaptic functions. Analog type memristors provide enormous possibilities in implementing analog bionic devices and human sensory perception systems.

Benefiting from the features of simple structure, rapid switching speed, high-density integration, and process compatibility with CMOS of memristors, the applications of CD-based memristors have been widely explored. They are mainly focused on information storage encryption and artificial neural networks [59,95]. As a novel memory device, CD-based memristors are promised to fulfill the low power consumption, high endurance, and non-volatile requirements of future memory devices.

With the continuous development of the Internet, the amount of data is growing exponentially. Therefore, there is a higher demand for energy efficiency and processing speed of data-centered tasks [33,55]. The shortcomings of traditional computers are limiting the growth of the current Internet because of some problems like von Neumann bottleneck and declining Moore's Law [33]. CD-based memristors are considered as the most promising candidates for the next generation of non-volatile memories owing to their fast access speeds, non-volatile data storage, low power consumption and high density 3D integration.

Fan et al. [96] prepared Au/TPAPAM-GOQDs/ITO memristors (Fig. 9 a). The ternary storage performances of TPAPAM-GOQDs composite thin-film based memristors were explored. The three resistive states of the devices changed by continuously applying voltage, and this could correspond to the "write-read-erase-read-rewrite" process observed in digital storage devices. The ternary memory behaviors were demonstrated by write-read-erase-read test. The resistance values remain constant even after 612 switching cycles (read at −100 mV) (Fig. 9b). This indicated that the prepared memory devices displayed high reliability, offering much better assurance for applications in ternary rewritable memory devices.

Lin et al. [59] constructed Al/PVP-NCQDs/ITO memristors (Fig. 9c and d). The visual image memory function was demonstrated in a 9 × 9 array. Applying voltage pulses of different amplitudes to the encrypted memristors array and controlling the switching voltage by adjusting the UV irradiation time, different image information could be read out. As shown in Fig. 9e, the images of letters “L”, “I”, and “H” were successively inputted into the RRAM array, which underwent UV irradiation for 15, 10, and 5 min, respectively (Fig. 9e). Read pulses with different amplitude (0.6, 0.9, 1.2 V) were applied to the encrypted state (Fig. 9f), and (Fig. 9g) different images including image "L", image "LI" and image "LIH" were decrypted, separately. It illustrated that the organic memristor array could realize encrypted image storage. This study confirmed that CDs and their composite-based memristors could be employed in storage devices for encrypted image storage functions. It not only boosted the information storage density, but also exhibited the potential applications of information encryption systems.

Memristive artificial neural networks can able to simulate various neuromorphic learning and have attracted considerable attention from researchers [97,98]. Analog type memristors with properties like synapses in living organisms are used for artificial neural network studies. Under the action of voltage, the resistive switching of CD-based memristors changes continuously. This unique electrical property of nonlinear transmission is highly identical to synaptic behaviors in terms of structure and function. Therefore, it presents significant advantages over conventional computers in brain-like neuromorphic computation, such as digital recognition. Meanwhile, the neuromorphic computing system based on analog type memristors can contribute to faster and energy-efficient methods for neural network training [20,61].

Gao et al. [95] constructed Ag/HfO2/100 mesh GOQDs/Pt memristors to perform digital recognition techniques (Fig. 10 a). It was possible to recognize from "0″ to "9″ based on artificial neural network. As shown in Fig. 10b and c, the map of weighting parameters posts two training iterations, which corresponds to a digital recognition accuracy of 4.18 %. The weighted parametric map after 10 training iterations corresponds to a digital recognition accuracy of 49.95 %. After 500 training sessions, the accuracy of digital recognition was 90.91 %. Additionally, a circuit-based filter was designed based on the memristors. The cutoff frequency of the filter could be successfully adjusted by changing the output signal of the control circuit with the value of the memristor resistance. As shown in Fig. 10d, a high-pass filter was physically constructed and tested using the Ag/HfO2/100 mesh GOQDs/Pt memristors. The high pass filter circuit based on the memristors could be equivalent to an R(t)C(t) circuit. The resistance value of the memristors could affect the value of the equivalent resistance R(t) and the value of the equivalent capacitance C(t). The amplitude–frequency and phase–frequency response curves of the circuit were obtained by Matlab, as shown in Fig. 10e. The cutoff frequency was controlled within the appeal range: f c=(0.16−1.429 GHz). This work established a new foundation for memristors in the field of artificial intelligence.

On the basis of testing the LTP and LTD characteristics of synaptic memristors, Pei et al. [20] used Pd/CQDs/Ga2O3/Pt memristors to recognize handwritten digits (0–9) in the National Institute of Standards and Technology (MNIST) database and obtained simulation results. A single perceptual layer (SPL) based artificial neural network (ANN) was used for digital recognition. After 250 training iterations, the carbon CFs model synaptic memristors showed a digital recognition accuracy of 92.63 %. Therefore, this novel carbon CF-based memristive mechanism provided new ideas for realizing better bionic neuromorphic computations and applications.

The emergence of CD-based memristors provides a completely new approach to artificial neural network functions. At the same time, the possibility of preparing neural network computers with human brain-like functions is further explored.

In addition to the applications mentioned above, owing to the distinctive resistive characteristics of memristors, multifunctional devices integrated with mechanical, optical, and ferroelectric properties have attracted widespread attention [99,100].

Qi et al. [70] successfully integrated p-GaN/n-ZnO heterojunctions with CD-based memristors (CQDs inserted as local electric field modulators into HfO2-x-based memristors) (Fig. 11 a) to develop an intensity-modulated light-emitting diode (LED) (Fig. 11b). The luminous intensity of the LED was manipulated by tuning the limiting current according to the resistive transition state of the memristors (Fig. 11c). Simultaneously, uniform operation of the multi-stage storage of the memristors could be achieved (Fig. 11d). LEDs modulated in this way could provide a viable alternative to conventional thin-film transistors or CMOS with complex structures and technologies, enabling potential applications for low-cost and high-density LED displays.

This paper overviews the recent advances of CD-based memristors, including the memristive mechanisms, devices, and application fields of CD-based memristors. First, metal conductive filament mechanism, electron trapping and detrapping mechanism and oxygen vacancy conductive filament mechanism of memristors are described specifically. Subsequently, it is concluded that CDs with uniform size, favorable dispersion, high degree of crystallinity, and abundant surface defects are more beneficial to enhance the performances of memristors. For digital type memristors, CDs feature the local electric field enhancement effect, which allows the CFs to grow and migrate in a regular and directional manner. This substantially improves the stability, parameter fluctuation and power consumption of memristors. For analog type memristors, in addition to the local electric field enhancement effect of CDs, the abundant defect sites on the surface facilitate the trapping of electrons in the functional layer to reduce the tunneling distance between charges. This could promote the generation of analog behaviors with continuous variation in resistive switching, which in turn mimics a wide range of synaptic behaviors. CD-based memristors generally exhibit superior performances. They hold indispensable potential in information storage encryption and artificial neural networks. Nevertheless, the study of CD-based memristors is currently in the preliminary research stage, and there are still some challenges and limitations:
1)
Explore the composite memristive mechanism of CD-based memristors: The occurrence of resistive transitions in devices is normally accompanied by field-induced transport of electrons, ions and other charges. The migration of electrons and ions affects microscopic variations such as the distribution of elements and material structure in the local region of the functional layer. This can alter the material energy level band gap and charge transport process, thus affecting the formation and breakage of the conductive pathway. Most of the current research qualitatively analyzes the memristive mechanism through current-voltage curves. However, there is lack of evidence to further prove it. In this regard, some advanced research tools can be used to prove, such as the appropriate combination of in situ characterization techniques and device modeling to simulate and explore the evolution of conductive pathways.
2)
Optimization of functional layer materials: There are two ways to explore new functional layer materials. First, the optimal ratios are investigated on the basis of well-defined material elements. For example, the doping ratios of CDs and other resistive materials are further optimized to obtain the best performances of devices. Second, new functional layer materials are explored, such as doping halogen atoms. Various heteroatom doping can vary the surface chemical structure, energy level structure, and bandgap width of CDs, endowing CDs with unique performances. It provides a feasible way to improve the performances of memristors.
3)
Expanding the industrialization application of CD-based memristors: CD-based memristors are merely in the experimental stage in terms of encrypted image storage and artificial neural networks. There are a lot of tasks to be done before it can be applied to large-scale market. Especially in the aspect of stability, memristors still need to be continuously improved. Hence, it is beneficial to upgrade the performance of the device to promote the device towards industrialization.

Although the application of CDs and their composites in memristors still involves some issues to be overcome, the research on CD-based memristors is evolving progressively. It is necessary to continuously explore the factors affecting the performances of memristors and to clarify the mechanism of devices. Therefore, CD-based memristors are expected to promote further development and accelerate their practical applications.
